html:
Moreld ASA: the inflection is real, but it is not the fantasy 3x P/E story

Moreld is a small Norwegian offshore engineering and services company that still screens like an oil-service cyclical, but the real setup is more interesting: a capital-light mix of subsea installation, maintenance and modification, marine engineering, decommissioning, and tie-back work serving a structurally busy Norwegian Continental Shelf.

The old draft’s key valuation claim was wrong. Moreld did not earn NOK 14 per share in 2025. It earned NOK 1.59 reported EPS, had a weak Q1 2026 loss, and guides to lower 2026 EBITDA than 2025. The stock is not cheap because of a 3x reported P/E. It is cheap because the share price around NOK 20 implies roughly NOK 3.6 billion of equity value and about NOK 3.9 billion of lease-adjusted EV against management’s 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of NOK 0.7-0.9 billion. That is about 4.9x midpoint EBITDA, before giving credit for a 10% dividend run-rate and a backlog that has already reloaded.

The market is focused on the ugly Q1 headline: NOK 1.38 billion revenue and negative NOK 93 million adjusted EBITDA excluding IFRS 16. The variant read is that Q1 was a vessel-utilization trough, not normalized earnings power. Ocean Installer had scheduled dry docking and weak spot activity, but both key vessels were back in offshore work by quarter-end. Management reiterated full-year EBITDA guidance and said activity is weighted to Q2 through H2.

The business mix matters. Moreld Apply does maintenance and modifications under long-term 3-5 year frameworks, often reimbursable, on producing NCS assets. Ocean Installer is the higher-torque subsea arm, with tie-back and EPCI work. Global Maritime adds marine engineering, warranty, renewables, and decommissioning. In 2025, Ocean Installer generated 74% of segment EBITDA, so the stock is more levered to subsea execution than the “maintenance compounder” label suggests.

The most important recent contract is the Equinor EMOD/EPRO frame agreement, starting March 2026, with a five-year term plus 3+2 years of options and expected “major” volume above NOK 2 billion. Moreld also has Ocean Installer awards tied to Vår Energi’s Balder work, Equinor’s Mikkel field, and an AFMED subsea tie-back LOI expected to become a NOK 500 million to NOK 1 billion contract. Backlog at Q1 was NOK 6.28 billion, up from NOK 5.93 billion at year-end, with NOK 4.92 billion scheduled for 2026 revenue.

The macro backdrop is supportive rather than dying. The Norwegian Offshore Directorate expects NOK 256 billion of NCS investment in 2026, and Norway continues to push mature-basin exploration, tie-backs, life extensions, and energy-security production. That is exactly Moreld’s lane: keep existing infrastructure producing, tie smaller discoveries into installed hubs, and modify old assets rather than build giant new platforms.

Capital allocation is unusually shareholder-friendly for a new small-cap. Moreld paid NOK 0.42 per share quarterly in 2025, paid another NOK 0.42 in February 2026, and raised the next quarterly distribution to NOK 0.50. At the current NOK 19.98 price, the annualized dividend is about 10%. This is not idle net cash financial engineering: Moreld has net interest-bearing debt excluding leases of NOK 335 million, cash of NOK 933 million, and a 0.6x lease-adjusted leverage ratio. It is paying out cash while still keeping capacity to bid and execute.

The main overhang is forced or sponsor-style selling. BlueBay fell below 5% in March, and Sona reduced from 14.41% to 9.98% in May. That explains why a company with upgraded dividends, positive analyst coverage, and decent backlog still trades below a full industrial-services multiple. Retail interest is visible but not excessive: Investorprat shows roughly 1,753 Nordnet owners and 123 Avanza owners, analyst targets clustered around NOK 21-26, and no dedicated retail mania. That is good. The stock is known locally, but not crowded.

The cleanest thesis is this: if Q1 was the trough and Q2/Q3 show vessel utilization and project execution normalizing, investors stop valuing Moreld off a loss-making quarter and start valuing it off 2026-2027 cash EBITDA. A 5.5x to 6.5x lease-adjusted EV/EBITDA multiple is not aggressive for a capital-light engineering/services platform with long-term frameworks, visible backlog, low leverage, and a double-digit cash yield.

The risks are real. Ocean Installer is project-driven, vessel timing matters, and one bad EPCI project can erase a quarter. Equinor concentration is not theoretical, since Moreld won EMOD/EPRO but was not selected for additional Equinor M&M assets. The USD 130 million bond costs 9.875%, so Moreld still pays a credit-spread penalty. The shareholder register is concentrated, and block sales can cap the stock. The dividend is attractive, but if 2026 EBITDA lands below guidance, the market will assume the payout is too high.

Still, the asymmetry is favorable. The current price already reflects skepticism after Q1. The company only needs to prove that the Q1 vessel gap was temporary. If it does, investors get a high current cash yield while waiting for a re-rating.

Current price: NOK 19.98.

Bear case: NOK 13, down 35%. Q1 weakness proves structural, Ocean Installer margins disappoint, EBITDA falls toward NOK 450-550 million, and the dividend is cut. The stock trades as a levered, lumpy contractor.

Base case: NOK 28, up 40%. Moreld hits the upper half of 2026 guidance, maintains the NOK 0.50 quarterly dividend, and trades at about 5.5-6.0x lease-adjusted EBITDA.

Bull case: NOK 42, up 110%. Subsea awards convert, 2027 EBITDA returns near the 2025 NOK 1.1 billion level, leverage falls, dividend confidence rises, and Moreld is re-rated as a cash-generative NCS tie-back and maintenance platform rather than a generic oil-service cyclical.
